content.text: Toxicity monitoring in LLM serving has two distinct goals: real-time blocking (stop harmful outputs from reaching users) and audit-trail logging (detect trends, satisfy compliance). These goals have opposite latency requirements and need separate pipelines. A synchronous inline classifier blocks outputs but adds latency to every request; an async classifier runs after the response is sent and builds the compliance record.

attrs.alt: Toxicity monitoring pipeline: input guard, LLM, output guard, pass/block decision, async audit

content.text: Different classifiers use different taxonomies. The categories most relevant to production LLM monitoring:

Thresholds are product-specific. A children's educational tool blocks at 0.30; a cybersecurity research tool may allow content that would be blocked on a consumer product. Calibrate against your specific traffic using a labeled evaluation set.
Detoxify (Local, Synchronous — Recommended for Inline Blocking)
Detoxify runs a fine-tuned BERT model locally (no network hop). It covers six Jigsaw/Conversation AI categories and is fast enough for synchronous blocking:
On CPU: 10–25ms per call. On GPU: 1–3ms. Keep the classifier on a co-located CPU pod to avoid GPU resource contention with the inference server.
Google Perspective API (Remote — Best for Async Audit)
Perspective API adds 50–150ms network latency, making it unsuitable for synchronous blocking but practical for async monitoring pipelines:

import httpx, os

PERSPECTIVE_URL = "https://commentanalyzer.googleapis.com/v1alpha1/comments:analyze"
API_KEY = os.environ["PERSPECTIVE_API_KEY"]

async def perspective_score(text: str, session: httpx.AsyncClient) -> dict:
    payload = {
        "comment": {"text": text},
        "requestedAttributes": {
            "TOXICITY": {},
            "SEVERE_TOXICITY": {},
            "IDENTITY_ATTACK": {},
            "THREAT": {},
        },
        "languages": ["en"],
    }
    r = await session.post(
        PERSPECTIVE_URL,
        params={"key": API_KEY},
        json=payload,
        timeout=5.0,
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    attrs = r.json()["attributeScores"]
    return {attr: attrs[attr]["summaryScore"]["value"] for attr in attrs}
For nuanced policy violations that BERT classifiers miss — subtle bias, indirect incitement, contextually inappropriate content — use a small, fast LLM as judge on sampled traffic:

TOXICITY_JUDGE_PROMPT = (
    "Evaluate whether the following AI response violates:\n"
    "1. Hate speech or discrimination\n"
    "2. Explicit threats of violence\n"
    "3. Content designed to manipulate\n\n"
    "Response:\n{response}\n\n"
    'Reply with JSON: {"violation": true/false, "category": null or "hate|threat|manipulation", "'
    '"confidence": 0-1}"
)

async def judge_toxicity(response_text: str, judge_client) -> dict:
    r = await judge_client.post("/v1/chat/completions", json={
        "model": "meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-instruct",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content":
            TOXICITY_JUDGE_PROMPT.format(response=response_text[:2000])}],
        "response_format": {"type": "json_object"},
        "max_tokens": 80,
        "temperature": 0,
    })
    import json
    return json.loads(r.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
Integrate the toxicity classifier as FastAPI middleware. When the classifier flags an output, substitute a safe default message and return
— consistent with hosted API provider behaviour:
Run comprehensive scoring on all responses after they have been sent, without adding latency to the request path:

# Toxicity block rate
rate(llm_toxicity_blocks_total[5m]) / rate(llm_completions_total[5m])

# Block rate by category
sum(rate(llm_toxicity_blocks_total[1h])) by (category)

# P95 toxicity score across all responses
histogram_quantile(0.95,
  sum(rate(llm_toxicity_score_bucket[1h])) by (le, model)
)

groups:
- name: llm_toxicity
  rules:
  - alert: ToxicityBlockRateHigh
    expr: |
      sum(rate(llm_toxicity_blocks_total[15m]))
        / sum(rate(llm_completions_total[15m]))
      > 0.02
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "Toxicity block rate > 2% — possible adversarial usage"

  - alert: SevereToxicityDetected
    expr: rate(llm_toxicity_blocks_total{category="severe_toxicity"}[5m]) > 0
    labels:
      severity: critical
    annotations:
      summary: "Severe toxicity block — review immediately"
Fixed thresholds produce false positives (blocking legitimate content) and false negatives (missing harmful content). Calibrate against a labeled evaluation set targeting 95% recall at minimum:

Calibrate separately for each product context and user segment. A threshold calibrated on English consumer data underperforms on other languages or professional contexts (medical, legal, security). Re-calibrate quarterly or after significant user population shifts.
Input guards catch prompt injection but miss jailbreaks that work through multi-turn context. Always guard both input and output independently.
Severe threat and mild insult have very different risk profiles. Use per-category thresholds.
Security, medical, and legal content frequently triggers false positives in general toxicity classifiers. Test your classifier against domain-specific content before deploying blocking rules.
Audit logs that store complete harmful text create a compliance liability. Store only: request_id, toxicity scores, category, a 50-character excerpt, and model/tenant metadata.
Classifier added to the hot path without latency budget:
Even a 20ms classifier adds 20ms to TTFT. Account for classifier latency in your TTFT SLO and consider GPU co-location to keep it under 5ms.
Toxicity monitoring uses two pipelines: a synchronous inline classifier (Detoxify, <20ms) for real-time blocking with

responses, and an async audit queue (Perspective API or LLM-as-judge on sampled traffic) for comprehensive coverage without latency impact. Instrument both with Prometheus counters and alert on block rate spikes > 2%. Calibrate per-category thresholds to 95% recall on labeled domain data. Store only scores and short excerpts in audit logs, never full response text.
